content.text: Why Indexing Matters for Vector Search

A brute-force vector search computes the exact distance from the query vector to every stored vector. At 1 million vectors of 1024 dimensions, that is 1 billion float multiplications per query — roughly 200–400 ms on a modern CPU. For real-time agent responses the latency budget is 50 ms or less. Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms trade a small recall penalty for 10–100× speed gains by building an index structure that narrows the search to a small candidate set.
Two algorithms dominate production vector databases:

(Inverted File Index). HNSW is the default in Qdrant, ChromaDB, and Weaviate. IVF is used in Faiss and is the basis for Pinecone's proprietary index. Understanding both lets you configure them correctly and interpret recall trade-offs.

HNSW builds a multi-layer graph. At the top layer a small subset of nodes are connected by long-range edges — these enable fast coarse navigation. At the bottom layer (Layer 0) every node participates and connections are dense and local. A search starts at the entry point in the top layer, greedily descends to the nearest neighbor at each layer, and refines the result in the densely-connected base layer.

attrs.title: HNSW layers: long-range at top, dense at base

content.text: — number of bidirectional edges per node at each layer. Default 16. Higher values improve recall but increase RAM and build time quadratically.
— dynamic candidate list size during build. Default 100. Higher values mean a better-quality graph at the cost of slower indexing.
(search) — candidate list size at query time. Larger ef → higher recall but slower queries. Can be set per-query without rebuilding the index.

from qdrant_client import QdrantClient
from qdrant_client.models import VectorParams, Distance, HnswConfigDiff, SearchParams

client = QdrantClient(":memory:")

# Create collection with tuned HNSW
client.create_collection(
    collection_name="docs",
    vectors_config=VectorParams(size=1024, distance=Distance.COSINE),
    hnsw_config=HnswConfigDiff(
        m=16,               # edges per node — 16 is a good default up to ~500k vectors
        ef_construct=100,   # higher quality graph at cost of build time
        full_scan_threshold=10_000,  # brute-force below this; faster for small collections
        on_disk=False,      # keep HNSW graph in RAM for sub-millisecond queries
    ),
)

# Search with elevated ef for high-stakes retrieval
hits = client.search(
    collection_name="docs",
    query_vector=query_vec,
    limit=10,
    search_params=SearchParams(hnsw_ef=128),  # override per-query
)

# Measure recall at different ef settings (benchmark helper)
def benchmark_hnsw_ef(client, collection, queries, ground_truth, ef_values):
    results = {}
    for ef in ef_values:
        recalls = []
        for q_vec, true_ids in zip(queries, ground_truth):
            hits = client.search(
                collection_name=collection,
                query_vector=q_vec,
                limit=10,
                search_params=SearchParams(hnsw_ef=ef),
            )
            retrieved = {h.id for h in hits}
            recall = len(retrieved & set(true_ids)) / len(true_ids)
            recalls.append(recall)
        results[ef] = sum(recalls) / len(recalls)
    return results

# ef=10 → Recall@10≈0.92, ef=64 → 0.97, ef=256 → 0.999 (typical for m=16)
IVF clusters the vector space using k-means into
Voronoi cells. Each vector is assigned to its nearest cluster centroid and stored in that cell's inverted list. At query time only
cells are searched instead of all cells, reducing the number of distance computations from N to approximately N/nlist × nprobe.

import faiss
import numpy as np

d = 1024          # embedding dimension
nlist = 1024      # number of Voronoi cells (√N is a good heuristic for N vectors)
nprobe = 32       # cells to search at query time — nprobe/nlist ≈ recall trade-off

# Build IVF index
quantizer = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)          # exact search for the quantizer step
index = faiss.IndexIVFFlat(quantizer, d, nlist)

# Train on a representative sample (required before adding vectors)
train_data = np.random.rand(50_000, d).astype("float32")
index.train(train_data)
assert index.is_trained

# Add all vectors
vectors = np.random.rand(100_000, d).astype("float32")
index.add(vectors)

# Search
index.nprobe = nprobe
query = np.random.rand(1, d).astype("float32")
distances, indices = index.search(query, k=10)

# IVF with product quantisation (IVF-PQ) — 8–32× less RAM
# Each 1024-dim vector compressed to m sub-quantizer codes
m = 16             # sub-quantizers (must divide d)
bits = 8           # bits per code (256 centroids per sub-quantizer)
index_pq = faiss.IndexIVFPQ(quantizer, d, nlist, m, bits)
index_pq.train(train_data)
index_pq.add(vectors)
index_pq.nprobe = nprobe

IVF requires a training step on a representative sample of the data. HNSW does not — it builds incrementally as vectors are inserted. This makes HNSW simpler to operate when the corpus grows continuously, while IVF works best for static or infrequently-updated corpora.
HNSW vs IVF: When to Use Each

1 million float32 vectors at 1024 dimensions occupy 4 GB of RAM — before the HNSW graph. Quantisation reduces storage at a modest recall cost. Qdrant supports scalar (int8) and binary quantisation natively.

from qdrant_client.models import (
    VectorParams, Distance,
    ScalarQuantizationConfig, ScalarType,
    BinaryQuantizationConfig,
    QuantizationConfig,
)

# Scalar (int8) quantisation: ~4× memory reduction, <5% recall loss
client.create_collection(
    collection_name="docs_int8",
    vectors_config=VectorParams(size=1024, distance=Distance.COSINE),
    quantization_config=QuantizationConfig(
        scalar=ScalarQuantizationConfig(
            type=ScalarType.INT8,
            quantile=0.99,     # clip top 1% of values to reduce quantisation error
            always_ram=True,   # keep quantised vectors in RAM even if originals are on disk
        )
    ),
)

# Binary quantisation: ~32× memory reduction, 10–20% recall loss — use with rescoring
client.create_collection(
    collection_name="docs_binary",
    vectors_config=VectorParams(size=1024, distance=Distance.COSINE),
    quantization_config=QuantizationConfig(
        binary=BinaryQuantizationConfig(always_ram=True)
    ),
)

# With binary: set rescore=True to re-rank top candidates with full float32 vectors
from qdrant_client.models import SearchParams, QuantizationSearchParams

hits = client.search(
    collection_name="docs_binary",
    query_vector=q_vec,
    limit=10,
    search_params=SearchParams(
        quantization=QuantizationSearchParams(
            ignore=False,     # use quantised index for candidate retrieval
            rescore=True,     # re-rank with original float32 (recovers recall loss)
            oversampling=3.0, # retrieve 30 candidates, rerank to top 10
        )
    ),
)

# Memory footprint comparison (1M × 1024-dim vectors):
# float32:   4 000 MB
# int8:      1 000 MB  (~4× reduction)
# binary:      128 MB  (~31× reduction)
retrieves Recall@10 within 1–2% of the full float32 index at 1/31st the memory. This is the recommended approach when running Qdrant with limited RAM.

LanceDB stores data in the Lance columnar format on disk (local or S3), building an IVF-PQ index that stays partially in memory. For large corpora that exceed available RAM, this avoids Qdrant's requirement to fit the HNSW graph in memory.

import lancedb
import numpy as np
import pyarrow as pa

db = lancedb.connect("~/.lancedb")

# Create table with schema
schema = pa.schema([
    pa.field("id", pa.string()),
    pa.field("text", pa.string()),
    pa.field("vector", pa.list_(pa.float32(), 1024)),
    pa.field("created_at", pa.float64()),
])
tbl = db.create_table("docs", schema=schema)

# Add vectors (appends to Lance files on disk)
import polars as pl
rows = [
    {"id": f"doc-{i}", "text": f"document {i}", "vector": np.random.rand(1024).tolist(), "created_at": 1700000000.0 + i}
    for i in range(1000)
]
tbl.add(rows)

# Build IVF-PQ index explicitly (recommended once > 10k rows)
tbl.create_index(
    metric="cosine",
    num_partitions=64,    # IVF cells — sqrt(N) heuristic
    num_sub_vectors=32,   # PQ sub-quantizers (must divide dimension)
)

# Hybrid search: vector ANN + SQL WHERE
results = (
    tbl.search(np.random.rand(1024).tolist())
       .where("created_at > 1700000500")
       .limit(10)
       .to_list()
)
Index tuning without measuring recall is guesswork. Recall@K is computed by comparing ANN results against brute-force ground truth on a held-out sample of queries.

import numpy as np
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient
from qdrant_client.models import SearchParams

def compute_recall_at_k(
    client: QdrantClient,
    collection: str,
    queries: np.ndarray,
    k: int = 10,
    sample: int = 200,
) -> float:
    """
    Compare ANN results to brute-force exact search.
    Returns Recall@k averaged over `sample` queries.
    """
    rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
    sample_queries = queries[rng.integers(0, len(queries), size=sample)]

    recalls = []
    for q_vec in sample_queries:
        # ANN (HNSW)
        ann_hits = client.search(collection, q_vec.tolist(), limit=k)
        ann_ids = {h.id for h in ann_hits}

        # Exact brute-force
        exact_hits = client.search(
            collection, q_vec.tolist(), limit=k,
            search_params=SearchParams(exact=True),  # bypass HNSW
        )
        exact_ids = {h.id for h in exact_hits}

        recalls.append(len(ann_ids & exact_ids) / k)

    return sum(recalls) / len(recalls)

# Usage
recall = compute_recall_at_k(client, "docs", query_vectors, k=10, sample=200)
print(f"Recall@10 = {recall:.3f}")  # target: > 0.95 for agent retrieval

# If recall < 0.95: increase hnsw_ef or m
# If latency too high: decrease hnsw_ef or enable quantisation

Run this benchmark before going to production and after any significant corpus growth (>2× vector count). A drop in recall without changing index parameters indicates the HNSW graph has become stale — trigger a reindex with higher
if Qdrant's background optimizer has not already done so.
Flat (Brute-Force) Index and When It Wins

Every vector database supports exact search — scanning all vectors and returning the true K nearest neighbors. Despite O(N) cost, it is the right choice for small collections and for validating ANN recall. Qdrant calls this
; ChromaDB uses brute-force below 10k documents automatically.

import faiss
import numpy as np

d = 1024
n = 5_000  # corpus size

vectors = np.random.rand(n, d).astype("float32")
query = np.random.rand(1, d).astype("float32")

# Exact L2 index — no training required
index_flat = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
index_flat.add(vectors)
distances, indices = index_flat.search(query, k=10)

# Exact cosine: normalize then use L2 (equivalent for unit vectors)
faiss.normalize_L2(vectors)
faiss.normalize_L2(query)
index_ip = faiss.IndexFlatIP(d)  # inner product = cosine after normalization
index_ip.add(vectors)
scores, indices = index_ip.search(query, k=10)

# Qdrant: exact search to verify ANN recall
from qdrant_client.models import SearchParams
exact_hits = qdrant.search(
    collection_name="docs",
    query_vector=query_vec,
    limit=10,
    search_params=SearchParams(exact=True),
)

# Rule of thumb:
# < 10k vectors  → brute-force (< 1ms at 1024d)
# 10k–1M         → HNSW default params
# > 1M           → HNSW tuned, or IVF-PQ for memory-constrained deployments

parameter in Qdrant's HNSW config (default 10 000) automatically switches to brute-force for collections below that size. This means you never need to choose — Qdrant handles the crossover internally.

Agent memory has a distinct workload pattern: frequent small inserts (new observations per conversation turn), low-latency reads (retrieval during tool calls), metadata filters (by user or session), and periodic bulk deletes (expiry). This differs from batch ML pipelines that insert millions of vectors once and read many times.
unless the collection exceeds 500k vectors — defaults give Recall@10 > 0.96 at < 1 ms latency for most agent memory sizes.
for filter fields. Without a payload index, Qdrant scans all points for the filter even when using HNSW. Create an index for any field used in
when running on < 8 GB RAM. The 4× memory reduction allows a 4× larger working set without spilling to disk.
) is cheaper than physical delete because it doesn't trigger HNSW graph maintenance. Run a hard-delete sweep hourly.

from qdrant_client.models import PayloadSchemaType

# Create payload index for filtered searches (run once after collection creation)
client.create_payload_index(
    collection_name="docs",
    field_name="user_id",
    field_schema=PayloadSchemaType.KEYWORD,
)
client.create_payload_index(
    collection_name="docs",
    field_name="created_at",
    field_schema=PayloadSchemaType.FLOAT,
)

# Now filtered search uses the index — sub-ms overhead instead of full scan
from qdrant_client.models import Filter, FieldCondition, MatchValue
hits = client.search(
    collection_name="docs",
    query_vector=q_vec,
    query_filter=Filter(must=[
        FieldCondition(key="user_id", match=MatchValue(value="alice"))
    ]),
    limit=10,
)

# Verify index is created
collection_info = client.get_collection("docs")
for field, schema in collection_info.payload_schema.items():
    print(f"  {field}: {schema.data_type}")
pgvector: HNSW and IVF inside PostgreSQL

pgvector brings both HNSW and IVF indexes to PostgreSQL as CREATE INDEX statements, letting you do vector search alongside SQL joins, transactions, and row-level security — without a separate vector database process.

-- Enable the extension
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;

-- Table: one float4[] column per embedding model
CREATE TABLE docs (
    id          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id     TEXT NOT NULL,
    text        TEXT,
    embedding   vector(1024),
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

-- HNSW index (pgvector >= 0.5)
-- m=16, ef_construction=64 are good defaults
CREATE INDEX docs_hnsw_idx ON docs
    USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops)
    WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 64);

-- IVF index (older pgvector, smaller RAM footprint)
-- lists = sqrt(N) heuristic
CREATE INDEX docs_ivf_idx ON docs
    USING ivfflat (embedding vector_cosine_ops)
    WITH (lists = 100);

-- Set probes for this session (IVF recall tuning)
SET ivfflat.probes = 10;

-- Set ef_search for HNSW recall tuning
SET hnsw.ef_search = 64;

-- Cosine ANN search with metadata filter
SELECT id, text, 1 - (embedding <=> $1) AS score
FROM docs
WHERE user_id = 'alice'
ORDER BY embedding <=> $1
LIMIT 10;
-- The <=> operator is cosine distance; <-> is L2; <#> is inner product (negative)
session parameter functions identically to Qdrant's
at the start of a connection, or in
for a global default. The tradeoff is identical: higher ef → better recall, higher latency.
tunes the recall–latency trade-off per query.
partitions vectors into Voronoi cells; requires training; excels for billion-scale static corpora with IVF-PQ compression.
(int8 or binary) cuts RAM 4–32×; binary + rescore recovers recall within 1–2% of float32.
provides on-disk IVF-PQ suited to corpora that exceed available RAM; Qdrant HNSW is the better default when RAM allows.
